concepts.title: Properties of adjoint of a matrix
concepts.conceptBody:
Adjoint of a Matrix

If $A=\left[ {{a}_{ij}} \right]$ be a n-squared matrix then the matrix $B=\left[ {{b}_{ij}} \right]$ such that ${{b}_{ij}}$ is the co-factor of the element ${{a}_{ji}}$ in the determinant $|\text{A}|$ is called the adjoint of matrix $\text{A}$ and is written as adj $\text{A}$.

In simple language we can say that adj. A is the transpose of the matrix formed by theco factors of elements of |A|.

Working rule for finding the adjoint of A. 

Write down the determinant IA I and the co-factors of various rows which will be columns of adj.A or replace each element in A by its co-factors and then take transpose to get adj.A.

Rule to write the cofactors of an element ${{a}_{ij}}$ 

Cross the row and column intersecting at the element ${{a}_{ij}}$ and the determinant which is left be denoted by D, then

$\left[ \begin{matrix} \text{ Cofactor of }{{a}_{ij}}=D & \text{ if }i+j=\text{ even } \\ =-D & \text{ if }i+j=\text{ odd } \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

Illustration 25

$A=\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \alpha & \beta \\ \gamma & \delta \\ \end{array} \right]$ find $\operatorname{adj}.\mathbf{A}$ and show that $\operatorname{adj}\cdot (\operatorname{adj}.\mathbf{A})=\mathbf{A}$

Co-factor of $\alpha$ is $\delta$ and co-factor of $\beta$ is $-\gamma$.

Co-factor of $\gamma$ is $-\beta $ and co-factor of $\delta$ is $\alpha$.

$\therefore \quad$ Matrix formed by co-factors is $\left[ \begin{matrix} \delta & -\gamma \\ -\beta & \alpha \\ \end{matrix} \right]$ …(1)

Adj. $A=$ transpose of matrix (1)

$=\left[ \begin{matrix} \delta & -\beta \\ -\gamma & \alpha \\ \end{matrix} \right]$ … (2)

You may see that $\delta ,-\gamma $ are the co-factors of first row of A and it forms the first column of adj.A, $-\beta ,\alpha $ are the co-factors of 2 nd row of $A$ and it forms the second column of adj.A

Rule for adjoint of 2 2 matrix: If A be 22 then adj. A is written by interchanging the elements of leading diagonal and changing the sign of the elements of other diagonal i.e. if $A=\left[ \begin{matrix} 3 & 4 \\ -5 & 7 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$ then adj. $A=\left[ \begin{matrix} 7 & -4 \\ 5 & 3 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$ ...(3)

i.e. elements 3,7 of leading diagonal have been interchanged and the sign of $4,-5$ in the other diagonal have been changed.

Properties of adjoint A.

The product of a matrix and its adjoint is commutative. 

(a) If A be n-rowed square matrix, then (adj.A) A $=\text{A}(\operatorname{adj}.\text{A})=|\text{A}|{{\text{l}}_{\text{n}}}$

$=|\text{A}|\left[ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \\ \end{array} \right]=\left[ \begin{matrix} |\text{A}| & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & |~\text{A}| & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & |~\text{A}| \\ \end{matrix} \right]$ … (I)

where |A| is determinant $A$ and ${{l}_{n}}$ is the n-rowed unit matrix.

Deduction (a) If A is a n-squared singular matrix i.e, $|A|=0$, then

$\text{A}(\operatorname{adj}\cdot \text{A})=(\operatorname{adj}\cdot \text{A})=\text{O}($ null matrix by $(\text{I})$

A matrix is said to be singular if its determinant is zero i.e. $|\text{A}|=0$

Deduction (b) $|\operatorname{adj}\mathbf{A}|=|\mathbf{A}{{|}^{\text{n}-1}}$ if |A|is not zero.

If clearly follows from above on taking determinants of both sides in (I) (a) that

$|\text{A}|$. ${{\left. |adj\text{A}|=|\text{A} \right|}^{\text{n}}}=|\operatorname{adj}\text{A}|$. $|\text{A}|$

$|\operatorname{adj}.\text{A}|=|\text{A}{{|}^{\text{n}-1}}$ provided $|\text{A}|$ is not zero.

If |A| is not zero then A is said to be non-singular matrix. 

Verification of the rule 

$(\operatorname{adj}.\mathbf{A})\mathbf{A}=\mathbf{A}(\operatorname{Adj}\mathbf{A})=|\mathbf{A}{{|}_{\text{n}}}$

$A=\left[ \begin{matrix} 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 0 \\ 3 & -1 & 4 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

$\text{ then Adj }A=\left[ \begin{matrix} 8 & -5 & -2 \\ -4 & -3 & 1 \\ -7 & 3 & -1 \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

Also $|\text{A}|=-11$ by calculation

$\therefore \quad$ A (adj.A) by actual multiplication

$=\left[ \begin{matrix} -11 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -11 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -11 \\ \end{matrix} \right]=\left[ \begin{matrix} |\text{A}| & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & |~\text{A}| & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & |~\text{A}| \\ \end{matrix} \right]$

$=\operatorname{diag}[|\text{A}|.|\text{A}|.|\text{A}|]$

Taking determinants of both sides, we get

$|\text{Aadj}.\text{A}|=\mid \text{ diag}\text{. }[|\text{A}|,|\text{A}|,|\text{A}|]$

or $|\text{A}|\,\,|\operatorname{adj}\text{A}|=|\text{A}{{|}^{\text{n}}}$

$\therefore |\operatorname{adj}\text{A}|=|\text{A}{{|}^{\text{n}-1}}$ provided

$|A|\ne 0$ i.e. $A$ is non-singular.

(b) Adj. $(\operatorname{Adj}\cdot \mathbf{A})=|\mathbf{A}{{|}^{\text{n}-2}}\mathbf{A}$ if $\text{A}$ be non-singular

Put Adj.A = B then

$\text{B adj}\text{.B }=|\text{B}|{{\text{I}}_{\text{n}}}=\operatorname{diag}.[|\text{B}|,|\text{B},\ldots |\text{B}\mid ]$

$\therefore \quad \text{A}(\text{B adj}\text{.B })=\text{ A diag}\text{. }[|\text{B}|,|\text{B}|\ldots |\text{B}|]$

$=|\text{B}|\text{A}$

Now put $B=\operatorname{adj}.A$ in $(I)$

$\therefore \quad |\text{B}|=|\operatorname{adj}\text{A}|=|\text{A}{{|}^{\text{n}-1}}$

$\therefore \quad A(\operatorname{adj}\cdot A)\operatorname{adj}\cdot (\operatorname{adj}\cdot A)=|A{{|}^{n-1}}A$

$|\text{A}|{{\text{I}}_{\text{n}}}\operatorname{adj}\cdot (\operatorname{adj}\cdot \text{A})=|\text{A}{{|}^{\text{n}-1}}~\text{A}$

$\therefore \quad \text{ }\operatorname{adj}\cdot (\operatorname{adj}\cdot \text{A})=|\text{A}{{|}^{\text{n}-2}}~\text{A}$

Particular Case: If A be 3 matrix, then

$\operatorname{adj}.(\operatorname{adj}\cdot \text{A})\text{ }=|\text{A}{{|}^{\text{n}-2}}~\text{A}$

$=|\text{A}{{|}^{3-2}}~\text{A}=|\text{A}|\cdot \text{A}$

Minor of $2=\left| \begin{matrix} -2 & 5 \\ 0 & 6 \\ \end{matrix} \right|=-12-0=-12$

Cofactor of $2={{(-1)}^{1+2}}(-12)=12[\therefore 2$ occurs in the 1 st row and 2 nd column]
